No one was even seriously injured. Every attack had been neutralized with precisely the force required and not a fraction more.

  • ‘Is this what you needed?’ he asked Thomas directly, his voice carrying infinite weariness. ‘To know you could choose to oppose me? You always could. Free will was never in question. The question is what you choose to do with it.’

  • ‘Send us home!’

  • ‘Home no longer exists for you. Time flows differently between worlds. Centuries have passed on Ispar. Your families are dust, their descendants wouldn’t recognize you, the world you knew is history or legend.’

The revelation broke something in Thomas. He’d suspected, but knowing was different. His next attack was pure rage, no strategy, just the need to make something else hurt as much as he did.

Asheron caught the blade with his bare hand, letting it draw blood—Empyrean blood that sparkled with contained power.

  • ‘I understand your pain. I carry the weight of every life disrupted by my portals. But that pain can become purpose. Your suffering can forge you into something capable of preventing others from suffering the same fate.’

  • ‘Pretty words from the architect of our misery.’

  • ‘Yes. I own what I’ve done. The question is: what will you do?’

3 The Schism

They gathered in the Nexus courtyard three days after the ambush, surrounded by the scorch marks and shattered crystal their attack had left behind. The air still smelled of burnt ozone. Khalid had set chairs around a stone table salvaged from the Empyrean ruins, but no one sat.

Thomas spoke first.

  • ‘Centuries. He said centuries.’ Thomas’s voice was hoarse. He hadn’t slept since the ambush. His hand rested on the knife at his belt, not as threat but as anchor—the only thing connecting him to the man who’d once carved wooden rabbits for a son who was now dust. ‘Mara. William. Everyone we left behind. Gone before we even learned what lifestones were.’

No one answered immediately. Marcus stood with his arms crossed, his five bodies arrayed behind him in a loose semicircle—a habit he’d developed, the distributed selves acting as both audience and counsel. Duulak sat on a broken pillar, Yasmin’s stone turning between his fingers. Maajid leaned against a crystalline column, his form only intermittently solid.

  • ‘We need to discuss next steps,’ Marcus said. ‘The Matriarch isn’t going to wait for us to process our grief. I’m proposing a unified command structure. Consolidated settlements. Shared resources, shared defense.’

  • ‘Under you.’ Thomas’s head snapped toward him.

  • ‘Under whoever is most qualified to—’

  • ‘Under you. Just say it, Marcus. You want an empire. Same thing you were building before the portals took you. Same thing you’d build on the surface of the sun if someone dropped you there.’

Marcus’s jaw clenched. Two of his bodies shifted their weight forward. The other three stayed perfectly still—a tell, Duulak noted, that his emotional control was fracturing across the distributed consciousness.

  • ‘What I want,’ Marcus said, each word measured, ‘is for people to stop dying. That requires organization. Structure. Leadership.’

  • ‘What I want,’ Thomas said, ‘is to go home. And don’t tell me there’s nothing to go back to. I’ll go back to the dirt where my son is buried if that’s all that’s left. That’s more than this place has ever given me.’

  • ‘You’d throw away what we’ve built here—’

  • ‘Built?’ Thomas stepped forward, and Marcus’s bodies tensed in unison. ‘We haven’t built anything. We’ve survived. There’s a difference. You just can’t see it because surviving is all you’ve ever known how to do.’

The silence that followed had weight. Elena, standing behind Thomas, put a hand on his shoulder. He shrugged it off.

Duulak cleared his throat.

  • ‘If I may offer a third perspective—’

  • ‘The one where none of this matters because it’s all data?’ Marcus didn’t look at him.

  • ‘The one where we’re arguing about the wrong question.’ Duulak stood, pocketing Yasmin’s stone. ‘Home or here, fight or build—those are responses. Before we choose a response, we need to understand what we’re actually responding to. The Harbinger Protocol, the lifestones, the Matriarch—we don’t know why any of it exists. We’re making decisions in the dark.’

  • ‘People are dying in the dark, Duulak. They don’t need understanding. They need walls and weapons.’

  • ‘Walls that Asheron just proved he can walk through whenever he pleases? Those walls?’

Thomas turned on Duulak, something ugly in his face.

  • ‘You stood there and watched. During the ambush. You stood there with your recording quills and your measurement instruments and you watched people die so you could write it down.’

  • ‘I—’

  • ‘You watched Sara resurrect for the third time in ten minutes and you took notes. Notes, Duulak.’

Duulak’s mouth opened, then closed. His hand found the stone in his pocket again.

Maajid pushed himself off the column. His form solidified, which was unusual—it meant he was choosing to be fully present.

  • ‘We’re not going to agree.’ His voice was quiet, almost gentle. ‘In every timeline I can see, this conversation ends the same way. We walk out of here in four different directions. So perhaps we should stop pretending otherwise and decide what the four directions look like.’

  • ‘I’m not giving up on—’

  • ‘No one is giving up on anything, Thomas. We’re admitting that four people who want four different things can’t pretend to want the same thing without tearing each other apart in the process.’

Thomas stared at him, then at Marcus, then at Duulak. His hand came off the knife. He turned and walked toward the Nexus gate, and Elena followed without a word. Halfway there he stopped, his back to all of them.

  • ‘The Forgotten,’ he said, without turning around. ‘That’s what we’ll call ourselves. Because the rest of you have already forgotten what was taken from us.’

He kept walking. Marcus watched him go, then exhaled through his nose and began dictating orders to Khalid in a low voice—troop movements, supply redistribution, the infrastructure of a civilization that would call itself the Builders.

Duulak looked at Maajid. Maajid shrugged—a strangely human gesture from someone who was barely human anymore.

  • ‘Seekers and Transcendent,’ Maajid said. ‘Has a ring to it. You study what reality is. We’ll study what it could become.’

Duulak nodded once, turned, and walked back toward the Seeker Encampment alone.

The factions weren’t entirely exclusive—people moved between them, and they still cooperated against common threats. But the unified resistance was over, replaced by four competing visions of what humanity should become.

In the months that followed, the division deepened along lines none of them fully understood. Families split between settlements. A Seeker researcher left for Paradox, unable to resist Maajid’s vision of expanded consciousness; her partner stayed, unable to abandon Duulak’s promise that understanding would save them. Two of Marcus’s best scouts defected to Haven, choosing Thomas’s desperate hope of return over Marcus’s pragmatic permanence. A woman named Katya walked between all four settlements, refusing to choose, and was eventually unwelcome in each.

The Virindi watched from their unseen vantage points, their thought-presence settling over the human settlements like frost on glass—there if you knew to look for it, invisible otherwise.

The pattern unfolds as projected. Division leads to specialization. Specialization leads to innovation. Innovation leads to transcendence or extinction. Both outcomes provide valuable data.

Deep in the southern tunnels, the Matriarch was also learning. She had lost soldiers in the ambush, though she had not lost the ambush itself—her objectives had never been what the humans imagined. She had wanted to see them fight their summoner, and they had shown her everything she needed: their coordination and its limits, their courage and its sources, the fracture lines along which they could be divided.

She began her preparations with the patience of a species that measured time in geological epochs. New soldiers gestated in chambers the humans hadn’t discovered yet. New strategies evolved through a billion simultaneous calculations. And in her vast, distributed mind, a new curiosity grew—not about humanity’s weapons or defenses, but about the strange, painful, irrational thing that made four damaged individuals choose four different paths rather than one efficient one.

She could not understand why bounded beings preferred division to unity. But she was determined to learn. And when the Matriarch determined to learn something, species tended to discover what it felt like to be studied from the inside.

Volume IV: The Crucible

Chapter 16 Evolution and Devolution

1 The Hybrid Horror

Six months after the schism, the scout’s report arrived at Fort Ironwood by emergency sending: Olthoi that moved like humans. Olthoi that used tools. Olthoi that spoke.

Marcus assembled a team of his best—veterans who’d faced every horror Dereth could produce and returned functional. Gaius, his lieutenant, who’d followed him through the portal within hours of his own crossing. Elena, borrowed from Haven, whose sharp eyes missed nothing. Three mages specializing in purification magic, in case what they found could be cleansed rather than destroyed.

The journey to Last Hope took four days. The settlement had been a mining outpost, fifty souls extracting crystal ore from a deposit the Olthoi had inexplicably avoided. Now Marcus understood why they’d avoided it: they’d been waiting.

Elena stopped a mile out, her face pale.

  • ‘Blood,’ she said. ‘Human blood. But also… something else. Something I don’t have words for.’

  • ‘The Olthoi?’

  • ‘Mixed together. The scents are…’ She shook her head. ‘They’re not separate. They’re combined.’

Last Hope’s walls still stood. That was the first wrong thing. Olthoi raids left rubble, not intact fortifications. The gates hung open, as if the inhabitants had simply walked out to greet their deaths.

Inside, Marcus found the second wrong thing: no bodies. Fifty people had lived here. Where were the corpses?

The answer waited in the mine.

They heard it before they saw it—a clicking that resolved into words, words that resolved into pleading. Human voices layered with insectoid harmonics, speaking from throats that were no longer entirely human.

  • ‘Help us,’ the darkness said. ‘Kill us. Please.’

Gaius lit a mage-torch, and Marcus wished he hadn’t.

They hung from the walls—or grew from them, it was impossible to tell. Human bodies twisted into impossible configurations, skin giving way to chitin in patches, limbs elongated or multiplied, faces frozen in expressions of agony that never ended because the lifestones wouldn’t let them die. Olthoi workers moved among them, adjusting, modifying, conducting experiments with the methodical patience of scholars.

One of the hybrids turned what had been a human face toward Marcus. He recognized her: Petra, the mining supervisor. He’d met her once, two years ago, when negotiating ore shipments. She’d laughed at his Roman formality, called him ”Commander Serious.”

She wasn’t laughing now. Her jaw had been restructured to accommodate mandibles, but her eyes—her eyes were still human, still aware, still screaming.

  • ‘Kill me,’ she begged, the words clicking and buzzing through a throat that couldn’t properly form them anymore. ‘Please, while I still remember my name.’

  • ‘What did they do to you?’ Marcus asked, though he could see exactly what they’d done.

  • ‘The Matriarch… she learns…’ Petra’s hybrid body convulsed as something moved beneath her skin. ‘She takes what makes us human… our creativity, our adaptability… adds it to her children. We’re still aware. Still ourselves. But also them. The hive song never stops. It’s so loud. It’s always so loud.’

Marcus drew his gladius. Forty-three years of command had taught him that some orders could only be given by example.

  • ‘Petra. Look at me.’ He waited until her too-human eyes focused on his face. ‘I’m sorry we didn’t come sooner. I’m sorry we couldn’t prevent this. I can’t undo what was done to you. But I can end it.’

  • ‘Will it… will it hurt?’

  • ‘No.’ A lie, but the only mercy he could offer. ‘Close your eyes. Think of home. Think of whoever you loved before the portals took you.’

  • ‘My daughter. Her name was—’

Marcus struck before she could finish. He didn’t want to know her daughter’s name. He didn’t want another ghost following him through the long watches of the night.

There were thirty-one hybrids in the mine. Marcus killed each one personally, looking them in the eyes, speaking their names when they could remember them, making it quick when the Olthoi modifications allowed for quick. Some begged for death. Some begged for more time. Some had fragmented so completely that they no longer knew what they were begging for.

The last hybrid was a child. Couldn’t have been more than twelve when the Olthoi took him. His transformation was more complete than the others—less human remaining, more hive—but his eyes still held the terror of a boy who didn’t understand why this was happening.

  • ‘I want my mother,’ he said, his voice almost entirely clicking now. ‘Can you find my mother?’

Marcus found her three bodies to the left. He didn’t tell the boy.

When it was done, when the mine held only corpses that would stay corpses because Marcus had destroyed them beyond what even lifestones could resurrect, he walked out into daylight and vomited until there was nothing left.

Gaius found him there, sitting against the mine entrance, gladius across his knees, staring at nothing.

  • ‘Commander—’

  • ‘She’s learning.’ Marcus’s voice was flat, empty. ‘The Matriarch. She’s not just fighting us anymore. She’s studying us. Taking us apart to see how we work. And when she’s finished studying…’ He stood, sheathed his blade with the automatic precision of decades of practice. ‘She’ll build something new. Something that has everything human that makes us dangerous, and everything Olthoi that makes them unstoppable.’

  • ‘What do we do?’

  • ‘We warn everyone. And then…’ Marcus looked back at the mine, at the settlement that had been called Last Hope. ‘Then we pray this was her only laboratory.’

It wasn’t.

The news spread through human settlements like wildfire, causing panic and rage in equal measure. The Builders demanded immediate fortification of all settlements. The Forgotten called for total war. The Seekers insisted on studying the hybrid process to understand and counter it. The Transcendent saw it as validation of their path—if the Olthoi could force evolution, humans should embrace it voluntarily.

2 Maajid’s Transformation

In Paradox, Maajid prepared for what he knew might be his final death—or his first true birth.

The settlement had grown strange over the years, its inhabitants increasingly comfortable with the boundaries of human experience. Buildings existed in configurations that hurt the eyes of visitors from other settlements. Time moved differently here, or seemed to—Maajid suspected that was perception rather than physics, but the distinction had become less clear.

Senna found him in the central chamber, surrounded by crystals he’d arranged in patterns that corresponded to nothing in Empyrean texts. His eyes were closed, but she knew he wasn’t sleeping. He rarely slept anymore.

  • ‘You don’t have to do this,’ she said. ‘There are other ways to fight the Matriarch.’

  • ‘Name one that doesn’t require becoming something like her.’ Maajid opened his eyes. They were still his mother’s eyes, dark and warm, but something flickered behind them now that hadn’t been there before. ‘The Olthoi are forcing humans to evolve through violation. I want to prove we can choose our own transformation. Voluntarily. On our terms.’

  • ‘And if you don’t come back?’

  • ‘Then I’ll have learned something about the limits of consciousness.’ He smiled, and it was almost the smile she remembered from the boy who’d laughed at the cosmic joke. ‘Either way, the experiment produces data.’

Celeste arrived from the Seekers the next morning, her scholarly skepticism barely concealing genuine concern. She’d brought recording equipment—crystals that would capture magical signatures, allowing later analysis of whatever Maajid was about to attempt.

  • ‘You’ll fragment,’ she warned. ‘Consciousness can’t maintain coherence across multiple incarnations. We’ve seen it happen to defenders who died too many times in the siege. They come back, but they’re not… whole.’

  • ‘Consciousness as we understand it, no. But what if understanding is the limitation? What if we’re meant to be more than singular?’

  • ‘You’re talking about deliberately inducing what the Olthoi are forcing on their victims.’

  • ‘No.’ Maajid’s voice was gentle but certain. ‘They’re forcing flesh to merge. I’m allowing consciousness to expand. The flesh will follow or be discarded.’

The ritual began at sunset.

Maajid had positioned seven lifestones in a circle around the central chamber—stones he’d persuaded, bargained, or stolen from other settlements, each attuned to his consciousness through months of careful preparation. The theory was elegant: die simultaneously in multiple locations, resurrect simultaneously in multiple locations, maintain awareness through all of it until the boundaries between selves dissolved.

The practice was agony.

He died the first time by his own hand, a knife across his throat, collapsing in the center of the circle. The resurrection came almost instantly—but not at one stone. At three. Three Maajids opened their eyes, three Maajids gasped with the memory of dying, three Maajids looked at each other and felt their sanity strain at the edges.

  • ‘Which one—’ one of them started.

  • ‘—am I?’ another finished.

  • ‘All of us,’ the third said. ‘And none of us. Keep moving.’

They died again. Poison this time, all three drinking simultaneously. The resurrections multiplied: five Maajids now, then seven as the original three died again, consciousness stretching across bodies like cloth pulled too thin.

Senna watched from outside the circle, her hands pressed against her mouth to keep from screaming. She saw him—saw them—flicker in and out of existence, sometimes solid, sometimes translucent, sometimes overlapping in ways that suggested they occupied the same space despite being meters apart.

By the second night, the boundaries between the Maajids had begun to blur. They no longer spoke in sequence but in chorus, their voices harmonizing from slightly different temporal positions. They moved in patterns that observers later described as choreographed, though no choreographer could have designed movements that accounted for past, present, and future simultaneously.

  • ‘The cardamom,’ they said together, their voices layered like music. ‘Mother’s hands crushing the seeds. That’s the anchor. That’s what makes us still Maajid and not just—’

They died again. And again. And again.

On the third morning, something changed. The seven Maajids stopped dying separately. Instead, they collapsed together, all of them at once, a synchronized cessation that the recording crystals would later show as a single event occurring in seven locations simultaneously.

There was a pause—five heartbeats, ten, a minute—where nothing existed in the circle except the crystals and the silence.

Then a single figure stood in the center.

It was recognizably Maajid but fundamentally changed. His edges seemed uncertain, as if reality hadn’t quite decided where he ended and the rest of existence began. When he moved, afterimages trailed behind him—not shadows but other versions of himself, other choices, other moments.

  • ‘I see it now,’ he said, his voice harmonizing with itself from slightly different temporal positions. ‘I used to call it a cosmic joke. But jokes have punchlines. This doesn’t end. Meaning exists in dimensions we couldn’t perceive—and the Matriarch understands this instinctively. That’s why she’s winning. She sees the pattern. She just doesn’t see what makes the pattern worth seeing.’

Senna approached him slowly, cautiously, as one might approach a fire that had grown too large for its hearth.

  • ‘Are you still Maajid?’

  • ‘I’m still the boy whose mother made cardamom tea when he was frightened.’ He smiled, and for a moment he was entirely present, entirely singular, entirely himself. ‘I’m also several other things now. But I haven’t forgotten who I was. I’ve just… expanded.’

  • ‘Can you teach others?’ Celeste asked, already calculating the implications.

  • ‘I can show them the door. Walking through it…’ He flickered, existing briefly in multiple positions. ‘That requires abandoning everything you think you know about being human. Most can’t do it. Most shouldn’t.’

Some of the Transcendent attempted to follow his path. Most failed, returning from death unchanged or not returning at all. A few succeeded partially, gaining abilities that shouldn’t exist but losing parts of themselves in the process. They became Paradox’s new guardians, beings that even the Olthoi avoided, sensing something fundamentally wrong about their existence.

3 Duulak’s Discovery

While others fought or transformed, Duulak pursued understanding with monomaniacal focus.

His laboratory occupied a chamber beneath the Seeker Encampment that had once been an Empyrean storage vault. The walls still hummed with residual energy, and the crystalline surfaces cast everything in a faintly blue light that made living tissue look like something already preserved in amber. Celeste had helped him organize it—specimen jars arranged by date and origin, notes pinned to crystalline shelves in his increasingly erratic handwriting, equations chalked on every flat surface until the room looked like the inside of a mathematician’s fever dream.

The hybrid samples occupied the far wall. He kept them in sealed containers, but some mornings he arrived to find the containers had shifted overnight, the tissue inside still responding to stimuli that should have killed it weeks ago. The samples pulsed. They grew. They reached toward each other through glass and crystal, tendrils of hybrid matter seeking connection with a persistence that made his skin crawl.

His research had revealed something unexpected: the hybridization process worked both ways. If Olthoi could absorb human traits, perhaps humans could absorb Olthoi capabilities.

Working with samples taken from hybrid corpses, he isolated the mechanism—a viral magic that rewrote genetic and magical patterns simultaneously. The Matriarch hadn’t developed it; she’d discovered it, possibly in the remnants of another species the Olthoi had assimilated on another world.

The night he confirmed this, his hands were shaking so badly he knocked over two specimen jars. The glass shattered. The hybrid tissue inside began immediately crawling toward each other across the stone floor. Celeste helped him contain it, her face pale but her hands steady where his were not.

  • ‘You’re certain?’ she asked.

  • ‘The mechanism is—well, it’s neutral, if I’m reading the samples correctly. A tool. We could potentially use it ourselves.’ He held up a slide, tilting it toward the crystalline light. Something moved within it—not alive, not dead, but transitioning. ‘The same process that creates those horrors in the mines could, theoretically, be directed. Controlled.’

He sent the findings to the others through magical sendings. The responses came back within hours.

  • ‘Become them.’ Marcus’s disgust cut through the magical sending. ‘No.’

  • ‘Not become. Borrow. Hive coordination, natural armor, vibration-sense—selectively adopted while maintaining individual consciousness. It’s theoretically possible that—’

  • ‘She thought that too.’ Thomas cut him off. ‘The Matriarch. Look at her now.’

  • ‘She was never human. We have individual will. That’s—I believe that’s the crucial variable she lacks.’

The debate raged for days through magical sendings. Finally, Duulak proceeded with a limited trial, using volunteers who were already dying from Olthoi venom, beyond even the lifestones’ ability to fully heal.

There were seven of them. Duulak knew their names—had made himself learn them, because Celeste had told him that if he was going to do this, he would remember the people he was doing it to. Joren. Katya. Samir. Fen. Elara. The twins, Bryn and Sera, who held hands through the entire process.

The application was agonizing—Duulak could see that in the way their bodies seized, in the sounds they made that he would hear for months afterward in the quiet hours. He monitored the transformation through his pattern-sight, watching human cellular structures negotiate with alien code, the equations of two species overlapping in configurations that shouldn’t have been possible.

The results were mixed but promising. The volunteers developed chitinous armor beneath their skin, invisible until needed. They could sense Olthoi presence through chemical signatures. Most importantly, they could sometimes understand Olthoi communication, providing intelligence that had been impossible to gather before.

But there was a cost. The volunteers reported dreams of the hive, whispers that grew louder over time. They remained human, remained themselves, but with an asterisk that grew larger each day.

Duulak documented everything. His notes were meticulous, his analysis rigorous, his conclusions hedged with appropriate caveats. And when he was done writing, he sat alone in his laboratory and held Yasmin’s stone, turning it between fingers that were beginning to look less like fingers and more like instruments of measurement, and tried to remember whether he’d crossed a line or merely identified its location.

Chapter 17 The Final Gambit

1 The Matriarch’s Ultimatum

One year after the hybrid horrors were discovered, the Matriarch did something unprecedented: she communicated directly with humanity. Not through attack or action but through words, delivered by a hybrid that retained enough humanity to speak but was wholly under her control.

The message was delivered simultaneously to all major settlements:

  • ‘The song of your species intrigues us. Individual notes creating accidental harmony. We offer integration without dissolution. Become part of the great song willingly, retain your consciousness within our unity. Refuse, and we will take what we need, leaving only husks.’

  • ‘Never,’ was the unanimous human response, though each faction meant it differently.

But the Matriarch’s follow-up message was more targeted. She reached into their sleep.

Marcus woke in all five bodies simultaneously, drenched in sweat. The dream had been so vivid it took him minutes to confirm he wasn’t still inside it—a vision of his soldiers, every one of them, standing in a field of golden light with no pain in their eyes. No lifestone scars. No flinching at loud noises. Whole, in a way resurrection never made them. And behind them, vast and warm, the hive’s embrace: Your soldiers die and resurrect endlessly, suffering without purpose. Join us, and their suffering ends. They become eternal, part of something greater than individual pain.

He wrote the dream down in his command log with steady hands. Then he read it back and realized his handwriting had shifted between the five copies—each body had recorded a slightly different version, as if the offer had resonated differently across his distributed consciousness.

Thomas dreamed of Mara. Not the fading memory of her—the reality, warm and solid, her hand on his cheek. William playing in a sunlit room that smelled of home. And a voice beneath it all, patient as geology: You seek return to a home that no longer exists. We offer a different return—to the unity all consciousness emerged from, where separation is illusion and loss is impossible. He woke with the carved rabbit in his fist, his nails driven into his palm hard enough to draw blood.

Duulak dreamed of answers. Every question he’d ever asked, resolved. Every equation, balanced. The Mechanism itself, spread before him like an open book. You seek understanding. We are understanding incarnate, billions of perspectives creating truth through consensus. Your questions would find answers in our collective knowledge. He woke reaching for his journal, the equations from the dream already dissolving, and felt a grief so sharp it surprised him—he hadn’t known he could still grieve for knowledge.

Maajid dreamed of stillness. For the first time since his transformation, he was one person in one place at one time. The relief was staggering. No flickering, no fractured states, no exhausting multiplicity. Just himself, singular, resting. You already hear our song, existing between singular and plural. Take the final step. Abandon the illusion of self for the reality of all-self. He woke in seven places at once, and in each one, he was weeping.

Each Harbinger was shaken by how precisely the Matriarch had identified their deepest desires and fears. She understood them, perhaps better than they understood themselves.

2 The Council of Harbingers

For the first time since the schism, the four Harbingers met in person, the Matriarch’s ultimatum forcing cooperation. They gathered at the Nexus of the Five Towers, the site of the failed ambush of Asheron, now recognized as neutral ground.

  • ‘Forced move.’ Marcus spread his hands on the table, tactical. ‘United, we hold. Divided, she takes us piece by piece.’

  • ‘United under what.’ Thomas didn’t bother making it a question. ‘Your acceptance? My rage? His experiments?’

  • ‘All of it.’ Maajid flickered. ‘She fears what she can’t predict. She knows unity—she is unity. Diversity confuses her.’

  • ‘That’s—I’d want to examine that claim more carefully. Could you elaborate?’

  • ‘Four paths to the same cliff. She can’t watch them all at once.’

The discussion continued through the night, old arguments resurfacing but tempered by necessity. Finally, Duulak proposed something that shocked them all:

  • ‘I’ve been considering—and I realize this will be unpopular—but we may need Asheron. Not as savior. As a knowledge source. His understanding of this world’s fundamental magic exceeds anything in the archives.’

  • ‘Our kidnapper.’ Thomas’s voice went flat. ‘You want us to work with him.’

  • ‘Use him. He wants us alive and evolving. We ensure the evolution serves us, not him.’

Marcus saw the tactical advantage immediately. Asheron’s power, properly directed, could provide the edge they needed. But he also saw the danger—Asheron’s help always came with hidden costs.

  • ‘We set conditions. He teaches us, provides resources, but doesn’t direct our actions. We maintain autonomy even in alliance.’

  • ‘He’ll never agree,’ Thomas argued.

  • ‘He will,’ Maajid said with certainty that came from perceiving multiple probability threads. ‘He’s more desperate than he shows. The Matriarch threatens his plans as much as our survival. He needs us to succeed, but on our terms now, not his.’

3 The Summoning

They called Asheron using a ritual Duulak had derived from Empyrean texts, combined with power drawn from the Nexus itself. It was not a polite request but a demanding summons, treating the archmage as resource rather than savior.

He appeared looking older than before, the weight of centuries visible in his bearing.

  • ‘You’ve grown,’ he observed, a mixture of pride and concern in his voice. ‘Faster than anticipated, though perhaps not in the direction intended.’

  • ‘Intentions are irrelevant now,’ Marcus stated flatly. ‘The Matriarch threatens to absorb humanity entirely. We need your knowledge to prevent it.’

  • ‘My knowledge comes with understanding of its cost. Are you prepared for that?’

  • ‘We’re prepared for survival,’ Thomas snarled. ‘Everything else is negotiable.’

Asheron studied each of them, his gaze lingering longest on Maajid’s transformed state.

  • ‘You’ve already begun the transformation necessary to resist her. Voluntary evolution rather than forced assimilation. But you’ve only scratched the surface of what’s possible.’

  • ‘Then teach us,’ Duulak demanded. ‘Show us how to become what we need to be without losing what we are.’

  • ‘I can show you the path. Walking it will change you irrevocably. You may defeat the Matriarch but become something your past selves would not recognize as human.’

  • ‘We’re already that,’ Maajid laughed, his voice echoing from multiple temporal positions. ‘The question is whether we become it with purpose or by accident.’

Asheron agreed, but with conditions of his own. The knowledge he would share could not be used against him or to attempt returning to Ispar. The transformation process, once begun, could not be reversed. And most importantly, humanity would have to choose its path freely, not be forced into it.

  • ‘Free choice,’ Thomas laughed bitterly. ‘In a world where all choices are constrained by your actions.’

  • ‘All choices are constrained by someone’s actions. The question is whether you’ll constrain your own or let others do it for you.’

Volume V: The Transformation

Chapter 18 The Rituals of Becoming

1 Asheron’s Revelation

In the depths beneath the Nexus of the Five Towers, Asheron revealed chambers that had been sealed since the Empyreans’ flight. Here, the magical energy was so dense it was almost tangible, streams of pure possibility flowing through crystalline conduits.

  • ‘This is where my people conducted their greatest experiments,’ Asheron explained. ‘Where they learned to transcend physical limitations, to touch the fundamental forces that shape reality. It’s also where they made their greatest mistake—opening the portal that brought the Olthoi.’

  • ‘And you want us to repeat their experiments?’ Marcus asked skeptically.

  • ‘No. I want you to complete them. The Empyreans fled before they could finish what they started. You have advantages they lacked—shorter lives that adapt quickly, the lifestones that allow learning through death, and most importantly, diversity of thought.’

He showed them the Synthesis Chambers, where consciousness could be separated from flesh and recombined in new configurations. The Evolution Pools, where magical energy could rewrite biological patterns. The Harmonic Resonators, where individual minds could temporarily merge without losing distinct identity.

  • ‘The Matriarch forces unity through dominance,’ Asheron continued. ‘But true power comes from voluntary synchronization, maintaining individual will while achieving collective purpose. It’s the paradox she cannot resolve because her nature doesn’t allow for paradox.’

  • ‘You’re talking about becoming like the Virindi,’ Duulak observed. ‘Thought beings that wear flesh when convenient.’

  • ‘No. The Virindi abandoned flesh and lost something crucial. You would maintain physical form while expanding beyond its limitations. Become more than human while remaining essentially human.’

Maajid, already partially transformed, understood immediately. He demonstrated by shifting his perception, showing the others glimpses of what he saw—probability threads, temporal echoes, the underlying music that reality danced to.

  • ‘It’s beautiful and terrible,’ he said. ‘You see everything—how small individual concerns are, how vast the patterns that connect all things. But you also see how crucial each small piece is to the whole. Remove one human, one choice, one moment, and entire futures collapse.’

2 The Four Paths Converge

Asheron laid out four different chambers, each configured differently, each designed to amplify a different aspect of consciousness.

  • ‘These transformations cannot be undone,’ Asheron warned. ‘You will gain power beyond imagination but lose the simplicity of singular existence. You will always be apart from standard humanity, bridges between what they are and what they might become.’

  • ‘We’re already apart,’ Thomas said quietly. ‘Every death, every resurrection, every day in this alien world has separated us from what we were. At least this separation has purpose.’

The rituals would take weeks, each Harbinger undergoing their transformation in sequence while the others maintained watch. The process would be agonizing, ecstatic, and ultimately irreversible.

3 The Transformation Begins

Marcus went first, entering the Synthesis Chamber with soldier’s discipline. The process split his consciousness into five parts, each inhabiting a separate body created from stored magical energy. At first, the sensation was maddening—five sets of eyes seeing different things, five minds thinking different thoughts yet knowing they were all one person.

  • ‘Focus on purpose, not perception,’ Asheron guided. ‘You are not five people but one person with five perspectives. Let them flow together like streams joining a river.’

It took days for Marcus to achieve synchronization. When he emerged, he moved with perfect coordination, five bodies acting as one will. He could hold five different conversations simultaneously, fight five different battles, exist in five different places while maintaining singular purpose.

  • ‘It’s like being a legion unto myself,’ he said, all five voices speaking in harmony. ‘I understand now why the Olthoi are so effective. But I also see their weakness—they have unity without individuality. I have both.’

Thomas’s transformation was more painful, not physically but emotionally. As the Memory Keeper, he experienced every moment of loss, every farewell never said, every home left behind by every human who’d come through the portals. Thousands of lives flooded through him, each carrying their own grief and hope.

  • ‘I can’t hold it all,’ he gasped, tears streaming down his face. ‘Too much loss, too much pain.’

  • ‘Don’t hold it,’ Asheron advised. ‘Let it flow through you. You are not a container but a channel. The memories pass through but don’t define you.’

When he finally stabilized, Thomas had aged visibly, his hair white, his eyes carrying depths that hadn’t existed before. But he also radiated a strange peace.

  • ‘I know now why we can’t go back,’ he said. ‘I carry ten thousand memories of Ispar, and in none of them is there room for what we’ve become. But I also carry the seeds of what we’re building here. Every settlement, every friendship formed, every child born on Dereth—we’re creating new homes, new memories. The loss is real, but so is the gain.’

Duulak’s transformation was the most dramatic physically. His body became a conduit for magical energy, his skin developing patterns that looked like equations written in light. He could see the flow of magic like others saw color, could reach out and adjust reality’s parameters within limited scope.

  • ‘Everything is mathematics,’ he laughed, the sound tinged with mania. ‘Every spell, every thought, every heartbeat—all following patterns that can be understood, predicted, manipulated. The Matriarch is just a very complex equation. And equations can be solved.’

But the cost was severe. Duulak could no longer fully return to normal perception. He saw the world always in terms of its underlying patterns, making simple human interaction challenging. Beauty became wavelengths, love became chemical reactions, hope became probability calculations.

In his pocket, the obsidian stone from Yasmin’s courtyard had become something else. He could see it now as a lattice of crystalline structures, a probability cloud of carbon atoms held in configuration by forces he could finally name. But he couldn’t remember why it mattered. He knew, abstractly, that a woman had given it to him. That the woman had been important. That the stone was supposed to anchor him to something he was losing.

He held it anyway. Not because he remembered why, but because the pattern of holding it had become part of his behavior, and patterns persisted even when meaning didn’t.

Maajid’s final transformation was the strangest. He entered the Evolution Pool already partially transformed, and for three days, nothing visible happened. He simply floated, suspended in crystalline energy, while his consciousness fragmented and reformed in patterns none of them could perceive.

When he finally opened his eyes, Thomas was the first to notice something wrong.

  • ‘His hand,’ Thomas whispered. ‘Look at his hand.’

Maajid lifted his left hand, and it existed in multiple states simultaneously. Not blurred—that would imply uncertainty. Instead, it was perfectly clear in each state, but the states overlapped: a hand reaching up, a hand falling down, a hand clenched, a hand open, all occupying the same space without contradiction.

He touched the wall of the chamber, and where his fingers made contact, the stone flickered between versions of itself—ancient and new, whole and crumbling, present and possible.

  • ‘Does it hurt?’ Duulak asked, his pattern-sight struggling to parse what he was seeing.

Maajid laughed, and the sound came from four directions at once, each version slightly out of sync with the others.

  • ‘Pain requires a single point of reference. I don’t have that anymore.’ He looked at his own flickering hand with something like wonder, something like grief. ‘When I try to remember being one thing, the memory splits. I remember being seventeen and afraid. I remember being ancient and tired. I remember dying and I remember never having been born. All of it is true. None of it is more true than the rest.’

He reached into his pocket—or tried to. His hand passed through the fabric, then caught on it, then had always been holding the cardamom pods that his mother used to grind every morning.

The scent hit him from seventeen years ago and from this moment and from a future where the pods had crumbled to dust. He crushed one between fingers that existed in multiple states, and the smell anchored him—briefly, painfully—to a single version of himself.

  • ‘I can still remember her,’ he said, and his voice was singular for just a moment. ‘When I smell this, I’m only one person. The boy who left home and never came back.’ The moment passed, and he was multiple again, flickering. ‘But holding onto that is like trying to hold water. It leaks through the spaces between my states.’

He looked at the others with eyes that perceived them from angles that shouldn’t exist.

  • ‘I spent years calling existence a cosmic joke,’ he said from everywhere and nowhere at once. His voice held something it hadn’t before—not amusement, not bitterness, but a kind of tender gravity. ‘But jokes are deflection. What I was avoiding was this: we’re not the punchline and we’re not the setup. We’re the space where meaning happens, even if we can’t see why. Consciousness isn’t a joke the universe tells itself. It’s the universe learning to care.’ He flickered, and for a moment all his states aligned into something that almost looked like peace. ‘That’s harder to laugh at. But it’s also harder to dismiss.’

And then—for one heartbeat that lasted forever—Maajid saw it.

The Mechanism.

Not the word. The thing itself. Or rather, a shadow of the thing itself passing across his fragmented consciousness like a whale passing beneath a boat—vast, dark, gone before he could comprehend what he’d glimpsed.

It wasn’t what he expected. It wasn’t order or chaos, not the equations Duulak sought or the unity the Matriarch craved. It was simpler than all of those. And it was prior to all of those. The question beneath all questions, the fact that made facts possible.

He couldn’t hold it. His mind wasn’t shaped for holding it. No mind was. The Empyreans had been right: to understand the Mechanism fully would require standing outside existence entirely, and there was no outside.

The moment passed. Maajid’s states scattered again, the insight dissolving like morning frost. He was left with only an impression—like waking from a dream that had explained everything, knowing only that you’d understood and that the understanding was gone. What lingered was not knowledge but sensation: warmth on the back of his neck, gentle and vast, the way his mother’s hand had felt when he was small and frightened. Something that held without grasping. Something that let go without leaving.

  • ‘What did you see?’ Duulak asked. His pattern-sight had registered something massive passing through Maajid’s consciousness, but he couldn’t parse what.

Maajid was quiet for a long moment. His voice, when it came, was small. Almost human again.

  • ‘I can’t tell you. Not because I won’t—because the words don’t exist. It’s like…’ He trailed off, his form flickering. ‘It’s like asking what silence sounds like. The question is shaped wrong. But—’ He looked at Duulak with an expression that held both recognition and loss. ‘—I think that’s the answer. The Mechanism isn’t something you understand. It’s something you inhabit. We’re already inside it. We always were.’

Duulak stared at him. Then, slowly, nodded.

  • ‘That’s not helpful.’

  • ‘I know. But it might be true.’

Chapter 19 The Final Battle

1 The Assault on the Great Hive

With the four Harbingers transformed and united in purpose if not in method, humanity launched its assault on the Great Hive. It was not a conventional military attack but something unprecedented—a war fought on multiple levels of reality simultaneously.

Marcus’s five bodies led five divisions toward five tunnel entrances, each assault timed to the heartbeat of his distributed consciousness. The coordination was inhuman—shield walls raised simultaneously across miles, pilum launched in synchronized volleys, retreats called at identical moments when the Olthoi pressure became too great.

The first engagement went well. Too well. Three tunnel entrances fell within hours, the Olthoi guards swept away by forces they couldn’t predict.

  • ‘Something’s wrong,’ Thomas said, his memory-sight pulling at tactical patterns from a thousand previous battles. ‘They’re retreating too easily. Not adapting.’

  • ‘Perhaps we’ve achieved surprise—’

  • ‘The hive doesn’t get surprised. It calculates.’

The trap sprung at sunset. From the fourth tunnel, a wave of Olthoi emerged—not the soldiers they’d been fighting but something new. Larger. Armored with chitin that deflected even Duulak’s modified weapons. And silent, their communication hidden from the patterns he could usually read.

Two hundred soldiers died in the first minute. By the time Marcus had coordinated a fighting retreat, four hundred more had fallen.

That night, beside the resurrection pools, Thomas found one of the dead soldiers reconstituting. A woman named Helena, who’d been under his command for two years. Her body reformed perfectly, but when she opened her eyes, they held nothing but screaming.

She couldn’t speak. Couldn’t stop screaming. The lifestone had preserved her consciousness, but the manner of her death—torn apart while fully aware—had broken something that resurrection couldn’t repair.

Thomas held her until dawn. When she finally stopped screaming, she just stared at nothing, her lips moving in words no one could hear.

  • ‘We’re creating ghosts,’ Thomas told the others. ‘The lifestones bring back the body, but some deaths—some deaths break what’s inside.’

The next two nights were worse than the battle.

The resurrection pools cast their pale green glow across the camp, and in that light everything looked drowned. Thomas sat beside Helena, who had stopped screaming but hadn’t started speaking. She held his hand with a grip that would have broken bones if he’d still been fully human, and he let her, because this was the only thing he could offer that wasn’t a lie. The other soldiers who’d died and come back—the ones who’d come back wrong—sat in clusters near the pools, flinching at sounds, touching their own skin as if uncertain it was real.

He counted them. Fourteen, on the first night. Twenty-three, by the second. People whose bodies had reformed but whose minds had shattered against the memory of being torn apart, dissolved, eaten alive. The lifestones didn’t distinguish between a death that could be survived and one that couldn’t. They just rebuilt. What happened inside the rebuilt skull was nobody’s concern but the screaming person’s.

At the perimeter, Marcus stood in three bodies while the other two slept. He’d learned to stagger his rest this way—consciousness distributed across five forms meant he never had to be fully unconscious, and something in him no longer wanted to be. Sleep meant dreaming in five directions at once, and the dreams were starting to bleed between bodies.

He sat at the command table in his third body and wrote. The letters were short now. Dear family of Corporal Venn. Your daughter fought with extraordinary courage. She died holding the line at the fourth tunnel. She was resurrected and fights still. He didn’t write: She screams when anyone touches her. She can’t eat without choking. She asked me to let her stay dead next time, and I told her I couldn’t promise that.

His hand was steady. Perfectly steady. He noticed this the way you notice weather—distantly, as a fact about the world. Two years ago, writing these letters had cost him something. His hand had trembled. His throat had closed. Now the pen moved and the words formed and the grief was something he understood intellectually but could not locate in his body. He finished the letter, folded it precisely, and reached for the next blank sheet.

In the supply tent, Duulak held a fragment of the new chitin under a lantern, turning it in fingers that were beginning to look less like fingers. Celeste sat across from him, grinding alchemical compounds, watching him sideways.

  • ‘The crystalline structure adapts,’ he murmured, more to himself than to her. ‘See here—the lattice reorganizes in response to specific magical frequencies. It’s not just armor. It’s a learning material.’

  • ‘How do we break it?’

  • ‘We don’t break it. We teach it the wrong lesson. Feed it a pattern it can’t integrate—’

He trailed off. His eyes had gone distant, the equations surfacing behind them like fish in dark water. Celeste reached across the table and closed her hand over his wrist.

  • ‘Come back,’ she said.

He blinked. Looked at her hand on his wrist as though it were a fascinating specimen. Then, slowly, like a man remembering how gravity worked, he returned.

  • ‘The chitin,’ he said. ‘I think I can disrupt it. But I need—’

  • ‘Sleep. You need sleep.’

  • ‘Sleep is a pattern of neural deactivation that—’

  • ‘Duulak.’

He slept. Or what passed for sleep, in a mind where the boundary between waking thought and dream had become a permeable membrane. In the morning, the equations were waiting for him like patient animals.

By the third day, the Olthoi had learned. Their new soldiers moved in patterns that countered Marcus’s synchronized assaults, their chitin evolved to resist Duulak’s reality-bending. Every advantage humanity had developed, the hive had analyzed and countered.

But they hadn’t countered Maajid.

He slipped into the hive-mind’s edge like water seeping through rock, not attacking but questioning. What do you seek? he asked the distributed consciousness. What would satisfy the hunger?

The hive-mind recoiled from the paradox of his presence—an individual asking about collective purpose, a fragment pretending to be whole. And in that recoil, Maajid saw something unexpected.

Loneliness.

The Matriarch, vast and ancient, had been seeking for so long that seeking itself had become her identity. She didn’t want to conquer; she wanted to understand. But every species she assimilated became her, and once they were her, they couldn’t show her anything new.

  • ‘She’s like me,’ Maajid told the others that night. ‘Or like what I was. Seeking something she can never find because finding it would end the seeking.’

  • ‘That doesn’t help us defeat her.’

  • ‘Maybe we don’t need to defeat her. Maybe we need to offer her what she actually wants.’

On the fourth day, Thomas killed an Olthoi soldier with a technique he’d never learned.

It happened in the narrow tunnels beneath the hive’s second ring, where the fighting was close enough to smell the acid on the creatures’ mandibles. A massive drone lunged from a side passage, faster than anything that size should move, and Thomas’s body responded before his mind could. He dropped beneath the strike, pivoted on his back foot, and drove his blade upward through the gap between thorax plates at precisely the angle required to sever the nerve cluster beneath.

The drone collapsed. Thomas stood over it, breathing hard, and realized the footwork belonged to a woman named Saren who’d died at the first tunnel. He’d absorbed her memories with the rest. Now her muscle memory lived in his tendons, her combat instincts threaded through his reflexes, and she was dead and he was using her like a tool.

He threw up against the tunnel wall. Then he wiped his mouth and kept fighting, because the next wave was already coming, and Saren’s techniques worked.

That evening, Maajid drifted deeper into the hive-mind than he’d gone before. He let himself thin, spreading his fractured consciousness along the pheromone channels and neural pathways like smoke filling a cathedral. The Matriarch’s presence surrounded him—not attacking, not yet. Tasting. Curious.

And in the vast architecture of her distributed mind, he found chambers that held nothing but silence. Places where absorbed species had once existed as themselves before being dissolved into the collective. The Matriarch kept these empty rooms the way a grieving person keeps a dead child’s bedroom. She returned to them. She listened to the silence in them. She did not understand why the silence was different from all her other silences, only that it was, and that she could not stop returning.

You’re lonely, he thought, and felt her entire consciousness flinch.

He withdrew before she could close around him. But the knowledge changed everything.

That night, late, the four of them gathered around the tactical map in Marcus’s command tent. It was the only time all four were in one place, and the air felt different when they were together—charged, unstable, like the moment before lightning finds the ground.

Marcus moved the carved wooden markers across the map. Two divisions here. Flanking force through the western tunnels. Duulak’s modified weapons concentrated at the chitin-growth nodes.

  • ‘The third ring is the weakest point,’ Marcus said, his five voices overlapping slightly. ‘But we lose the tunnels if we commit there. Thomas?’

Thomas closed his eyes. Ten thousand memories of this terrain unfolded behind his eyelids—scouts who’d mapped these tunnels, soldiers who’d died in them, an Olthoi drone whose alien spatial sense he’d absorbed without meaning to. The geometry assembled itself.

  • ‘There’s a junction. Here.’ He placed a finger on the map without opening his eyes. ‘Sixty meters below the main passage. The chitin walls are thinner—older growth. If Duulak can weaken the lattice—’

  • ‘I can.’ Duulak’s translucent fingers were already tracing equations in the air above the map, probability structures flowering like frost on glass. ‘Seventy-three percent chance of structural failure if I apply a resonance cascade at these three points. But the margin—’

  • ‘Not seventy-three,’ Maajid said from three directions at once. His flickering form settled briefly, almost solid. ‘I can see twelve hours ahead in two of my states. She’ll reinforce the western node before dawn. Shift the third point here.’ He reached through the map—literally through it, his hand passing into the wood—and when he withdrew it, Duulak’s equations had rearranged. ‘Eighty-nine percent.’

For one heartbeat, they were a single organism. Four minds with four different ways of seeing the world, and the world made more sense through all four lenses than through any one. Marcus felt it—the wholeness, the click of complementary perception. Thomas felt it. Even Duulak, dissolving by degrees into mathematics, felt something that his equations couldn’t capture but his diminishing humanity still recognized.

Then Maajid flickered, his states scattering, and the moment broke like a soap bubble. They were four damaged people again, standing around a table in a tent that smelled of blood and lamp oil and fear.

  • ‘Same time tomorrow?’ Marcus said.

  • ‘If tomorrow cooperates,’ Maajid replied, already fading.

By the fifth day, something had shifted. Duulak’s reality-modifications were becoming harder to maintain—his pattern-sight was bleeding into his other senses, making it difficult to remember which version of events was real.

He tried to tell Celeste something important, something about the equations underlying consciousness, and realized he’d forgotten her name. She was just a pattern now, a set of variables that his expanded perception could model perfectly but no longer feel.

  • ‘Your hands are shaking,’ she said.

  • ‘Hands are irrelevant. The pattern beneath the hands is what—’

  • ‘Duulak. Your hands. Look at them.’

He looked. His fingers were translucent, equations visible beneath the skin where muscle should be. The transformation was continuing beyond what they’d planned, converting flesh to pure information.

  • ‘I’m becoming what I see,’ he said. ‘That’s the cost. The more I understand reality, the less I exist within it.’

Meanwhile, Thomas discovered he could no longer remember his son’s laugh. He could access ten thousand memories of children’s laughter from the humans he’d absorbed, but William’s specific sound—that unique, precious thing—had been overwritten by the collective.

He stood at the edge of the assault perimeter, clutching the carved rabbit, trying to remember something that had been the center of his universe. And found only absence where it had been.

  • ‘This is what we’re fighting for,’ he whispered to no one. ‘To not become this. To not lose ourselves the way I’m losing—’

He couldn’t finish. He didn’t know what he was losing anymore. Only that it mattered, and that he could no longer recall why.

The battle raged for seven days and seven nights. Thousands died and resurrected, the lifestones working overtime to maintain human forces. The Olthoi adapted to every tactic, evolved counters to every strategy, but couldn’t adapt to the fundamental unpredictability of four different approaches working in concert.

2 Confronting the Matriarch

The tunnel opened into a space that shouldn’t have existed.

The chamber was cathedral-vast, its walls carved from living crystal that hummed at frequencies below hearing and above thought. The air was thick—not with moisture but with meaning. Every breath carried pheromone-language so dense that Thomas staggered, ten thousand absorbed memories trying to translate simultaneously. Scent-words layered on scent-words: warnings, histories, taxonomies of pain, a chemical archive of every species the hive had ever consumed. The crystalline surfaces refracted light from no visible source, and the dimensions of the space shifted when you weren’t looking directly at them, corners folding into geometries that made Duulak’s equation-sight flare white.

And at the center, the Matriarch.

She was not one thing. She was a landscape of chitin and thought, her central mass filling the far end of the chamber like a dark cathedral within the cathedral. Compound eyes the size of tower shields arranged in clusters that tracked each of them independently. Her chitin shifted color as she processed their presence—deep black to iridescent violet to the blue-green of deep ocean, thoughts made visible on the surface of her body. Smaller creatures moved across and through her, drones no larger than a fist flowing in and out of ports in her carapace like blood cells through a heart, each one carrying information, each one an extension of her will. Where they emerged, her chitin glistened. Where they entered, it pulsed.

She was ancient. The scars told that story—gouges in her armor where species had fought back before being absorbed, ridges of regrown chitin layered over wounds that predated human civilization. One massive forelimb bore the marks of something that had burned her with energy so intense the chitin had fused into glass. She had survived that, too. She had survived everything.

The pheromone-language intensified, pressing against their minds like a hand against a door.

  • ‘You resist integration,’ she spoke through a thousand hybrid voices—not just Olthoi but fragments of absorbed species, tongues long dead, vocal cords that had belonged to beings who no longer existed as themselves. ‘Why? You could be eternal, part of something greater than your small selves.’

  • ‘My soldiers follow. Yours obey.’ Marcus’s five voices spoke as one. ‘Die as Marcus or live as swarm? Not a choice.’

  • ‘You are chaos. We are order. Order always prevails.’

  • ‘I’ve been studying your order,’ Duulak said, his equation-sight tracing her limits. ‘Fascinating structure, but—and I hope this doesn’t sound dismissive—you’ve been iterating on the same equation for eons. No new terms. We’re still introducing variables.’

The Matriarch’s chitin darkened to black. The small creatures flowing across her surface reversed direction, and the chamber’s hum dropped an octave.

Then the walls came alive.

Marcus moved first. All five bodies surged from different entry points—three he’d positioned in advance, two that had circled through side tunnels during the dialogue. Each carried different weapons, each attacked at a different angle, and for three seconds the synchronized assault was the most beautiful violence Thomas had ever witnessed. Five blades striking in concert, each body covering the others’ blind spots, the whole constellation of Marcus moving like a single hand closing into a fist.

The Matriarch caught two of them.

Massive forelimbs, faster than anything that size should move, closed around Marcus-Two and Marcus-Four. The chitin of her grip was adaptive—it softened to absorb the impact of their weapons, then hardened to crushing pressure. Marcus screamed from two throats simultaneously, the pain ricocheting across his distributed consciousness. The other three bodies stumbled, hands going to wounds that weren’t on their flesh. He could feel his ribs breaking in bodies twenty meters away, the air leaving lungs he couldn’t see.

Thomas was already moving, an arrow nocked and released in a motion that belonged to Saren, to Corporal Venn, to soldiers whose names he’d never known. The arrow found the joint between the Matriarch’s forelimb segments and sank deep. She didn’t flinch. She’d absorbed species that used projectiles. She’d evolved past caring about arrows a million years ago.

But Thomas’s second arrow carried one of Duulak’s equations embedded in its shaft—a resonance formula that taught the Matriarch’s chitin to vibrate at its own shattering frequency. The forelimb cracked. Marcus-Two tumbled free, gasping, three ribs broken and his left arm hanging useless.

Marcus-Four was not so fortunate. The Matriarch brought him to her face—those vast compound eyes, each facet reflecting a different version of his terrified expression—and the pheromone-language washed over him like a wave. Join. Become. You are already many. Become more.

His consciousness buckled. For a terrible moment, Marcus felt the pull of the hive-mind through his fourth body—felt how easy it would be to let the boundaries dissolve, to stop being five and become billions. To stop carrying the weight of command. To stop writing letters to the families of the dead.

Thomas’s voice cut through it.

  • ‘Marcus. The letters. Who writes them if you don’t?’

Marcus-Four drove a blade into the Matriarch’s eye.

She shrieked—a sound that existed in dimensions beyond hearing, a pheromone-scream that sent every drone in the chamber into spiraling panic. Marcus-Four fell, caught by Marcus-Five, and the five bodies staggered back together, reforming their constellation. Two were badly hurt. All five were shaking.

Duulak attacked on a different level entirely. His translucent fingers wove equations into the air, reality-modifications that should have disrupted the Matriarch’s biological processes—slowing her neural transmission, introducing errors into her chemical communication, making her chitin forget its own structure. For ten seconds, it worked. The Matriarch’s movements became jerky, uncoordinated. Her color-shifting chitin froze in a confused mosaic.

Then she adapted. Her biology rewrote itself in real-time, incorporating Duulak’s equations into her immune response the way a body incorporates a vaccine. His modifications became part of her. His weapons became her armor.

  • ‘She’s learning my mathematics,’ Duulak breathed. Blood was running from his nose, his translucent fingers flickering between solid and equation. ‘Every attack I make teaches her how to counter the next one. I’m making her stronger.’

Maajid was the last to engage, and the most dangerous. He didn’t attack—he contradicted. His fractured existence created paradoxes in the Matriarch’s hive-logic: he was inside her mind and outside it, part of the collective and rejecting it, a member who was also an invader. Each paradox forced her consciousness to branch, to hold two incompatible truths simultaneously, and she was not built for incompatibility. She was built for unity.

The distortions rippled outward from Maajid’s flickering form. Drones fell dead where the paradox-waves struck them, their simple minds unable to process the contradiction. The crystalline walls cracked. The Matriarch’s chitin blazed through colors so fast it became white, then black, then something the eye refused to register.

But each paradox cost him. His states scattered further, his form becoming less a person and more a wound in causality. Blood—or what had replaced blood in his transformed body—ran from his eyes in streaks that existed in multiple timelines. He was tearing himself apart to tear her apart.

  • ‘Maajid, stop!’ Thomas reached for him and his hand passed through three versions of Maajid’s shoulder before catching a fourth. ‘You’ll annihilate yourself.’

  • ‘Annihilation requires a single self to annihilate.’ Maajid’s voice came from everywhere, each word arriving from a different state. ‘I have spares.’

The Matriarch screamed again, but this time the sound was different. The pheromone-language beneath it was not rage or pain. It was something Thomas recognized, because he carried ten thousand versions of it in his borrowed memories.

It was grief.

She closed around them—all of them. The chamber contracted, chitin walls flowing inward, the Matriarch’s body unfolding to reveal its true scale as she wrapped herself around the four Harbingers like a fist closing. Drones poured from every surface. The crystal hummed at a frequency that made bones vibrate and thoughts blur. In the shrinking space, Marcus’s bodies were pressed together, Duulak’s equations dissipating against living walls, Thomas’s arrows useless at this range, Maajid’s paradoxes rebounding off a consciousness too vast to fully destabilize.

They were losing. The air was pheromone-thick, the hive-mind pressing against their thoughts like water against a dam. Marcus could feel his five selves beginning to blur at the edges. Duulak’s equations were becoming her equations. Thomas was drowning in alien memories that the Matriarch was feeding him deliberately—millions of perspectives, billions of absorbed lives, all crying out in her voice: See? See how much I contain? See how alone it makes me?

And Thomas, buried in that avalanche of alien consciousness, saw something that stopped him cold.

A memory. Not human. Not Olthoi. Something older, from a species absorbed so long ago that even the Matriarch had forgotten its name. A memory of a creature standing at the edge of an ocean on a world that no longer existed, watching the waves, feeling the particular loneliness of being the only thing in the universe that knew it was watching waves. The ache of consciousness. The terrible privilege of awareness.

The Matriarch had kept this memory. Out of all the billions she’d consumed, she’d kept this one, turning it over and over in her vast mind the way Thomas turned the carved rabbit in his pocket. She didn’t understand why it mattered. She only knew that it did.

  • ‘Stop,’ Thomas said. Not shouted. Said. Quietly, firmly, with the exhaustion of a man who’d been fighting for years and had just realized the enemy was crying. ‘Everyone stop.’

The chamber stilled. Marcus lowered his weapons across five bodies. Duulak let his equations fade. Maajid drew his scattered states closer together, the paradoxes softening to mere improbabilities.

The Matriarch’s grip loosened. Not releasing them—waiting.

  • ‘You cannot destroy us,’ the Matriarch said, and her voice had changed. The thousand hybrid tongues were quieter now, less chorus and more congregation. ‘We are billions. You are thousands.’

  • ‘Don’t need to destroy you.’ Thomas’s voice was flat, exhausted. ‘Just stop. Both sides.’

  • ‘What terms?’

  • ‘Boundaries. Your hives. Our children.’ His voice caught. ‘Children grow up without fear.’

  • ‘Impossible. We expand or die.’

  • ‘Then expand elsewhere.’ Maajid’s form rippled, showing her visions of other worlds. ‘Universe is infinite. This rock is small. Why claw over scraps when banquets wait in every direction?’

3 The Resolution

The final resolution came not through victory but through transcendence. Asheron, who had been observing, stepped forward with an offer that shocked everyone.

  • ‘I can open portals to other worlds, uninhabited ones. The Olthoi can expand without conflict. Humanity can build without threat. But it requires agreement from both sides.’

Thomas moved before anyone could react. His bow was useless here, but the knife at his belt was in his hand, pressed against Asheron’s throat before the Empyrean could finish speaking.

  • ‘You could always do this.’ Thomas’s voice was ice. ‘You had this option from the beginning.’

  • ‘Thomas—’ Marcus started.

  • ‘Don’t.’ The knife pressed harder. A thin line of blood appeared—Empyrean blood, dark as wine. ‘He brought us here. Chose us. Watched us die over and over, watched us lose everything we loved, watched us become this—’ He gestured at his own white hair, his eyes that held ten thousand griefs. ‘And now he tells us it was all necessary? That we had to be broken before he could fix things?’

Asheron didn’t move. Didn’t defend himself.

  • ‘You’re right,’ Asheron said quietly. ‘I could have sought this solution earlier. I chose not to. I needed you to become what you’ve—’ He stopped. The careful sentence he’d been constructing collapsed. ‘I used your suffering. To forge what I needed.’

  • ‘Admit it. Say the words.’

  • ‘I am responsible for every death. Every—’ He stopped again. Started over. The parallel constructions of fifteen centuries of Empyrean rhetoric were failing him. ‘I selected you. Knowing. I watched your transformations knowing they were irreversible and I—’ His voice broke, and the sound it made was not human but something older, a grief that had been running for millennia. ‘—I am monstrous. What I’ve done is unforgivable. I knew that when I did it.’

  • ‘Then why should I forgive you?’

  • ‘You shouldn’t.’

The chamber was silent except for the hum of crystalline energy. Even the Matriarch’s vast presence had stilled, watching.

  • ‘I won’t,’ Thomas said. ‘I can’t. My son is a stranger to me now. My wife mourned me and moved on. Every parent here who lost children, every child who lost parents—that blood is on your hands. And you knew. You calculated. You decided our suffering was an acceptable price.’

  • ‘Yes.’

  • ‘So here’s what happens.’ Thomas’s knife hand was steady. ‘We do this. We save humanity. We become your bridges. But you don’t get to be redeemed. You don’t get to be the wise mentor who made hard choices. You get to live with what you did, knowing that at least one of us will never, ever forgive you for it. Every time you look at me, you’ll see exactly what your necessary choices cost.’

He lowered the knife. Stepped back. The thin cut on Asheron’s throat healed slowly—Empyrean physiology was remarkable—but both of them knew the wound Thomas had inflicted wouldn’t heal as easily.

  • ‘The portals require anchors on both sides,’ Asheron continued, his voice stripped of its former authority. ‘Beings who can maintain the connection. The four of you, transformed as you are, can be those anchors. And the Matriarch, vast as she is, can maintain her end.’

The Matriarch stirred. Through her thousand hybrid voices, something shifted—curiosity replacing hostility.

  • ‘Mutual imprisonment,’ she observed.

  • ‘Mutual opportunity,’ Duulak corrected. ‘We become bridges between worlds, allowing controlled expansion and exchange without forced assimilation.’

  • ‘I have sought understanding for eons,’ the Matriarch said slowly. ‘Every species I absorb becomes mine, and mine cannot surprise me. But you four—’ Her attention swept across them, tasting their transformed consciousnesses. ‘You remain other, even when connected. You maintain boundaries I cannot breach. Perhaps… perhaps that is what I have been seeking. Not absorption but exchange. Not unity but relationship.’

  • ‘You’re saying you’d accept limits,’ Marcus said. ‘After millions of years of expansion.’

  • ‘I am saying I might try. For the first time. To see what grows in the space between us rather than within me.’

The debate continued for hours after that, hammering out specifics: boundaries, territories, exchange protocols, enforcement mechanisms. The Matriarch agreed to withdraw her forces to the southern continent, establishing clear boundaries. Humanity would maintain the north and central regions. Portals would be opened to three uninhabited worlds, allowing Olthoi expansion without human conflict.

The agreement nearly collapsed once. The Matriarch demanded the right to maintain scout-drones in human territory—passive observers, she insisted, incapable of harm. Marcus refused. Five voices, five simultaneous refusals, each one colder than the last.

  • ‘No eyes in our cities. No drones in our fields. You want to observe, you ask. Every time.’

  • ‘Observation without presence is meaningless. How can I trust boundaries I cannot see?’

  • ‘The same way we trust you won’t dissolve the accord the moment it inconveniences you.’ Marcus leaned forward in three bodies. ‘Faith. The thing neither of us has any reason to offer.’

The silence stretched. The Matriarch’s chitin cycled through dark purples, the color of deep uncertainty. Then Duulak spoke, his voice thin, his translucent fingers tracing a small equation in the air between them.

  • ‘Exchange observers. Human emissaries in hive territory. Olthoi liaisons in ours. Not drones—individuals. Beings who can be known, questioned, held accountable. Relationship, not surveillance.’

The Matriarch considered this for a long time. Her compound eyes tracked the equation hovering above Duulak’s hand—the mathematics of mutual vulnerability, expressed as a balanced function.

  • ‘Individuals,’ she said, tasting the word. ‘I have not made an individual in a very long time.’

  • ‘Then it will be new for both of us,’ Duulak said.

She agreed. The accord held.

The four Harbingers would serve as living treaties, their transformed nature allowing them to monitor and maintain the agreement. They would be neither fully human nor Olthoi but something between, ensuring neither side could break the accord without consequence.

  • ‘Standing watch forever,’ Marcus said quietly. ‘Could be worse duty.’

  • Thomas touched the carved rabbit in his pocket. ‘Could be worse. Could be alone.’

Asheron stood apart from them now, diminished somehow, the weight of Thomas’s unforgiveness visible in his posture. He had achieved what he set out to achieve. He had saved what could be saved. And he would carry the cost of that salvation for however many centuries remained to him.

The Matriarch’s presence receded into the crystal depths, taking her vast hunger with her. Above, the first pale light of dawn touched the ruined spires.

Marcus surveyed what remained of his people—battered, transformed, but alive. Duulak was already translating, hands moving through sigils that would anchor the agreement in reality itself, the obsidian stone from Yasmin’s courtyard clutched in his other hand like a talisman against the equations consuming him.

Elena found Thomas before he could retreat fully into himself. She was bloodied, exhausted, her bow broken and her quiver empty—she’d survived the final assault with a knife and sheer refusal to die permanently. She didn’t say anything. Just stood beside him, shoulder to shoulder, the way she’d stood beside him through three years of impossible war.

  • ‘You’re different now,’ she said finally. ‘I can feel it. You’re carrying more than you were.’

  • ‘Ten thousand memories.’ His voice was hollow. ‘Ten thousand griefs. I can’t tell anymore which ones are mine.’

  • ‘The rabbit.’ She nodded toward his pocket. ‘That one’s yours.’

It was such a simple thing to say. Such an obvious thing. But somehow it helped.

Thomas stood apart, his expression unreadable, the Virindi presence flickering at the edge of perception. Elena remained nearby—not intruding, but present. The way she’d always been present.

For a moment, something passed between Thomas and the thought-beings—not words, not concepts, but recognition. They had studied him because he could still want things. He had used them because they could still know things. And somehow, in the using and being used, both had changed.

We still cannot feel what you feel, the Virindi transmitted. But we remember now why feeling mattered.

Thomas touched the carved rabbit in his pocket. The wood was worn smooth now from years of handling, the details of the ears and tail nearly gone. He’d carved it for William, but William had never received it. He’d kept it to remember William, but William’s laugh was gone now, overwritten by ten thousand other children’s laughter.

The rabbit wasn’t a connection anymore. It was a record of loss.

But maybe that was enough. Maybe carrying the shape of what he’d lost was its own form of faithfulness. He couldn’t go home. He couldn’t remember his son’s voice. But he could still carve. And tomorrow, or next year, or in a century, he would make another rabbit—for a child here, on Dereth, one who had never seen Ispar but might still find comfort in something small and handmade.

He didn’t answer the Virindi. He didn’t need to.

Maajid stood apart from the others, his form flickering between states as the dawn light passed through him in ways it shouldn’t have been able to. Senna stood a few paces behind him, close enough to ground him, far enough to give him room. She’d been there through the final assault—he’d seen her in three of his states, fighting with the quiet competence of someone who’d given up on safety and settled for purpose.

He was watching Thomas and Elena, watching Marcus writing one final letter—this one not a condolence but an order of the day, the first of a peacetime command—watching Duulak’s translucent hands weave the accord into the fabric of reality.

Then the wind shifted, and from somewhere—the ruined kitchens below the spire, perhaps, or a timeline where his mother was grinding spices in a courtyard in Gharu’n—the smell of cardamom reached him.

His states collapsed. For three heartbeats, he was one person. One young man who had left home and never come back, standing in the wreckage of a war he’d helped to end, smelling his mother’s kitchen across an impossible distance. His eyes were wet. His hands were solid. His shadow fell in only one direction.

  • ‘Maajid?’ Thomas had noticed. He always noticed.

  • ‘It’s nothing.’ The states were already returning, his form beginning to scatter. But he held on—held onto the singularity of himself for one more breath, one more heartbeat. ‘Just remembering what kitchens smell like.’

He let go. The moment passed, and he was multiple again, fractured and flickering. But he was smiling in every state, and the smile was the same in all of them.

It was not an ending. It was not a beginning. It was something in between—a threshold crossed, with all the uncertainty that implied.

The Call had been answered. What came next was theirs to decide.

END
